coredata.dc:title: Spatial confinement-enabled full-color emission from carbon dots via controlled sp2 domain assembly in spray pyrolysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lan, Lin
Gan, Zhenfeng
Ma, Yuan
Bao, Rui
Kong, Xin
Liu, Liang
Yi, Jianhong
coredata.dc:description: The development of high-performance solid-state fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) for advanced optoelectronics is hindered by two fundamental limitations: (i) severe emission quenching in aggregated states, and (ii) unresolved structure-property relationships governing emission tunability. We report a transformative spray pyrolysis approach that overcomes these barriers through the continuous, large-scale synthesis of solid-state emissive CDs with precisely controlled emission from 440 to 630 nm. By combining real-time thermal profiling with optimized precursor stoichiometry, we achieve an industry-compatible 81.2 % synthesis yield. Advanced structural and spectroscopic analyses reveal that the exceptional optical properties arise from an elegant Si-O-Si network architecture, where quantum-confined sp2 carbon domains synergize with engineered surface states to generate size-dependent emission, while in situ-formed silicon‑oxygen networks provide three-dimensional spatial confinement that suppresses π-π stacking. The material exhibits unprecedented environmental stability, maintaining >95 % emission intensity after 60 days without protective encapsulation. Our work establishes a new platform for the industrial manufacturing of high-performance luminescent nanomaterials through integrated process-structure-function engineering, opening avenues for sustainable optoelectronic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solid-state fluorescence
Tunable full-color emission
Light-emitting diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and chemical origins of fluorescence modulation
Optical properties of full-color solid-state fluorescent carbon dots
Luminescence mechanism of full-color solid-state fluorescent carbon dots
LED devices based on full-color solid-state carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-e) TEM, HRTEM (upper right), and particle size distribution (lower right) of B -, G -, Y -, O - and R-CDs. (f) EDS-TEM of G-CDs.
(a) Raman spectrum. (b) XRD. (c) FTIR. (d) XPS full spectrum. (e) Relative elemental content.

(a-e) 3D mapping of excitation emission of B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs. (f) Normalized optimal PL emission spectra of five types of CDs. (g) Normalized optimal PL excitation spectra for five types of CDs. (h) UV–vis spectra of five types of CDs. (i) TRPL spectra of five types of CDs.

(a) Schematic diagram of full-color solid CDs synthesized by spray pyrolysis. (b) PL emission mechanism of full-color solid-state CDs. (c) Illustrates the luminescence mechanism of CDs using R-CDs as an example. (d) Schematic diagram of electronic transitions and energy transfer in full-color solid-state CDs.

(a) CIE chromaticity coordinates of W-LED. (b) CIE chromaticity coordinates of B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs. (c-h) PL emission spectra of white, blue, green, yellow, orange and red CDs-based LEDs (the illustrations show actual photos of the operating status of various LED devices). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Spatial confinement-enabled full-color emission from carbon dots via controlled sp2 domain assembly in spray pyrolysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Material Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Material Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Material Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650093, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of high-performance solid-state fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) for advanced optoelectronics is hindered by two fundamental limitations: (i) severe emission quenching in aggregated states, and (ii) unresolved structure-property relationships governing emission tunability. We report a transformative spray pyrolysis approach that overcomes these barriers through the continuous, large-scale synthesis of solid-state emissive CDs with precisely controlled emission from 440 to 630 nm. By combining real-time thermal profiling with optimized precursor stoichiometry, we achieve an industry-compatible 81.2 % synthesis yield. Advanced structural and spectroscopic analyses reveal that the exceptional optical properties arise from an elegant Si-O-Si network architecture, where quantum-confined sp2 carbon domains synergize with engineered surface states to generate size-dependent emission, while in situ-formed silicon‑oxygen networks provide three-dimensional spatial confinement that suppresses π-π stacking. The material exhibits unprecedented environmental stability, maintaining >95 % emission intensity after 60 days without protective encapsulation. Our work establishes a new platform for the industrial manufacturing of high-performance luminescent nanomaterials through integrated process-structure-function engineering, opening avenues for sustainable optoelectronic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The global lighting industry consumes 20 % of worldwide electricity, driving urgent demand for sustainable solid-state emitters. Conventional phosphor technologies face sustainability challenges: Rare-earth systems suffer from declining ore reserves and low quantum efficiencies, while lead-halide perovskites and Cd-based quantum dots present environmental liabilities and operational instability. These limitations highlight the imperative for developing earth-abundant alternatives that simultaneously achieve high efficiency, operational stability, and manufacturing scalability [1].

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising candidates, demonstrating remarkable progress in solution-phase applications: white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) with record 97 color rendering index (CRI) [2], full-color emission tunability [3], and matrix-embedded composites demonstrating 88.14 lm/W efficacy [4]. However, transitioning these solution-phase successes to solid-state operation reveals fundamental limitations: Aggregation-induced energy transfer and π-π stacking cause severe PL quenching [5,6].

Existing anti-aggregation strategies (e.g., alkyl chain functionalization or SiO2 encapsulation) compromise either charge transport or process scalability [7], while recent advances like Schiff base-derived CDs [8] and chitosan composites (CRI = 92) [9] remain limited by low yields (<50 %) and complex synthesis route.

Spray pyrolysis is an innovative synthesis platform that integrates rapid precursor pyrolysis with vapor-phase nucleation, thus enabling the production of non-agglomerated nanoparticles [10]. Compared with conventional methods such as microwave-assisted synthesis and hydrothermal approaches, this technique demonstrates distinct advantages in online continuous production capability while eliminating the need for subsequent complex purification procedures. The process exhibits high operational stability, which facilitates the consistent preparation of products with excellent homogeneity and reproducibility [11]. Using this principle, we have developed an enhanced coaxial spray pyrolysis system (residence time is about 3 s). This system can suppress the quenching caused by aggregation induced quenching (AIQ) through the formation of in-situ Si-O-Si network by coordinating the precursor composition and reaction temperature (300–600 °C), and achieve a productivity of 81.2 % without complex purification steps. The emission wavelength is continuously tuned from 440 nm to 630 nm. Synthetic CDs have a wide range of applications in multi-color LEDs and WLEDs, providing a scalable and sustainable strategy for solid-state lighting.

3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, ≥98 %), phloroglucinol (PG, ≥99.5 %), and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co. (Shanghai, China). Maleic acid (MA, ≥99 %) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm) was produced by a Milli-Q system (Millipore). All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

CDs were synthesized via a pneumatic nebulization-assisted spray pyrolysis system. The precursor solution (0.01 mol L−1) was prepared by dissolving MA and APTES in a 2:1 M ratio in 500 mL ultrapure water, followed by homogenization via ultrasonic treatment (10 min). The precursor was atomized at 1.5 mL min−1 using a 1.7 MHz ultrasonic nebulizer, with air gas flowing at 5 L min−1. Pyrolysis was conducted in a tubular furnace with precise temperature control. The resultant aerosols were collected on a water-cooled quartz filter (20 °C), washed sequentially with ethanol/water, and vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 12 h. For red-emitting CDs (R-CDs), 0.1 wt% PG was introduced into the precursor solution prior to pyrolysis at 600 °C (residence time about 3 s).

Field-emission SEM (FE-SEM, ZEISS Gemini 500) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20) with EDX mapping. XRD (Shimadzu XRD-7000). Surface chemical composition and bonding states were investigated: XPS (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). FTIR (Hitachi FTIR-8400 s). Raman (Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution). Optical properties were systematically analyzed: UV–vis (Shimadzu UV-3600). Steady-state PL (Hitachi F-2500). TRPL and PLQY were tested using Horiba Fluorolog-3 at the optimal excitation wavelength.

The CDs (B-, G-, Y-, O-, and R-CDs) were utilized as single-phosphor materials to fabricate multicolor LEDs. A homogeneous dispersion was prepared by ultrasonicating 0.5 mg of CDs and 0.25 mg PVP in ethanol for 10 min. The dispersion (50 μL) was drop-cast into an LED lamp cup, dried at 80 °C, and repeated four times to achieve a total volume of 200 μL. The dried phosphor layer was integrated with a 365 nm UV LED chip to assemble the devices. For white light emission, a composite of B-, G-, and R-CDs was deposited similarly. The emission properties of WLEDs were optimized by tuning the ratio, yielding stable white light with adjustable chromaticity under UV excitation.

Our spray pyrolysis platform enables unprecedented control over CD emission through three-dimensional parameter space (precursor ratio, temperature, additive). As shown in Fig. S1a, the PL intensity of CDs increased initially and plateaued at a 2:1 MA/APTES ratio, with no further enhancement at higher ratios. This trend arises from stoichiometric matching of MA (-COOH) and APTES (−NH2), promoting amide bond formation for surface passivation (reducing non-radiative centers) and siloxane network generation (suppressing π-π stacking and quenching). Excess MA (>2:1) introduces unreacted -COOH groups, inducing CD aggregation via protonation, disrupting the siloxane network through hydrogen bonding, and increasing deep-level defects, thereby limiting PL enhancement.

Control experiments highlighted the necessity of both precursors: APTES deficiency caused produce blue emitting CDs with a production rate of<20 % due to incomplete SiO network formation, while MA-deficient systems failed to produce fluorescent CDs. Thermal profiling established distinct synthesis regimes: Below 300 °C yielded non-fluorescent amorphous carbon, while temperatures exceeding 600 °C caused graphitization-induced PL quenching (Fig. S1b). This synergistic precursor interplay facilitated continuous chromaticity tuning under 365 nm excitation: Blue (B, 440 nm), Green (G, 520 nm), Yellow (Y, 555 nm), Orange (O, 595 nm). PG was introduced as a π-extending agent to induce emission red-shift in CDs. Its benzene ring acts as a conjugated backbone during carbonization (500–600 °C), extending the π-system via π-π stacking to reduce bandgap energy, while surface hydroxyl groups (-OH) dynamically regulate defect states through hydrogen bonding or esterification and form cross-linked structures via dehydration to suppress non-radiative loss. This synergy between conjugation extension and hydroxyl-mediated surface modulation enables precise fluorescence tuning, yielding red-shifted R-CDs. All the results in Table S1 show that the method delivered exceptional synthesis yields (81.2 % for Y-CDs), surpassing conventional solution-phase approaches.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and chemical origins of fluorescence modulation
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and microstructure of the synthesized full-color solid-state CDs were systematically characterized to elucidate the structure-property relationships underlying their fluorescence modulation [12].

TEM analysis revealed monodisperse spherical morphologies for all CDs (B-, G-, Y-, O-, and R-CDs), with particle diameters ranging from 2 to 10 nm ( Figs. 1
a-e ). Notably, a monotonic increase in average particle size was observed from B-CDs (1.75 nm) to R-CDs (4.25 nm), consistent with a size-dependent fluorescence red-shifting mechanism (Fig. S2) [13].

HRTEM imaging unambiguously identified the crystallographic evolution ( Fig. 1
a-e insets). Well-defined lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.25 nm (B-CDs), 0.23 nm (G-CDs), 0.215 nm (Y-CDs), and 0.21 nm (O- and R-CDs) were resolved, indexed to the (110), (002), (100) and (100) planes of graphitic carbon, respectively.

The structural evolution originates from precisely controlled carbonization dynamics. Elevating the synthesis temperature enhances molecular thermal motion and spatial confinement effects, synergistically increasing the nucleation rate and grain growth driving force. This thermodynamic control induces progressive carbon core densification, evidenced by the observed particle size increase and lattice contraction.

The structural parameters collectively demonstrate a quantum confinement-regulated fluorescence mechanism: Bandgap narrowing: Increased sp2 domain size reduces interband energy levels; π-electron delocalization: Lattice contraction expands conjugated systems; Synergistic effect: Cooperative action red-shifts emission wavelengths [14].

SEM confirmed the monodisperse spherical morphology, showing ultrasmooth surfaces without aggregation (Fig. S3). Complementary TEM-EDS elemental mapping ( Fig. 1
f) revealed homogeneous C/N/O/Si distributions, suggesting an APTES-derived Si-O-Si network. This architecture creates spatial barriers that effectively suppress π-π stacking between adjacent CDs, thereby inhibiting non-radiative decay pathways and enabling solid-state fluorescence.

Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 2a) was employed to quantify the structural ordering progression in CDs. The monotonic decrease in the ID/IG ratio from 1.66 (B-CDs) to 0.95 (R-CDs) indicates a pronounced enhancement in the ordering of sp2-hybridized carbon domains [15]. This trend aligns with the reduction of structural defects and improved lattice alignment during graphitization, which is further corroborated by the well-defined lattice fringes observed in HRTEM. The systematic enhancement of sp2‑carbon network ordering induces modifications in the electronic band structure, leading to a red shift in fluorescence emission from 440 nm to 630 nm, thereby enabling precise multicolor luminescence tuning.

The XRD patterns (Fig. 2b) demonstrate graphite-like structural ordering in the CDs. All samples exhibit broad (002) diffraction peaks within the range of 21.4°-23.4°, with a systematic shift toward higher angles observed from B-CDs to R-CDs. This angular progression reflects a reduction in the interlayer d-spacing, indicative of vertical compression between graphitic layers, alongside structural densification driven by high-temperature-induced rearrangement of sp2-hybridized carbon networks. The temperature-dependent peak migration aligns with TEM observations, which reveal confined-space carbonization effects characterized by vertically compressed layers (smaller d-spacing) and laterally extended conjugated domains (larger crystallite sizes). Such anisotropic structural evolution modifies electronic confinement effects, thereby contributing to the emission red shift.

The chemical composition and surface functionalization of the five CDs were systematically investigated by FTIR and XPS analyses. FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2c) identified consistent surface functionalization across all samples. A broad absorption band at 3428 cm−1 was attributed to the overlapping stretching vibrations of OH (hydroxyl) and NH (amine/amide) groups, indicating the presence of hydrophilic surface moieties. The peak at 2945 cm−1 corresponded to CH stretching vibrations in aliphatic or aromatic carbon structures. Two prominent absorption bands at 1698 cm−1 and 1629 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching modes of CO (carbonyl) and CN (amine/amide) bonds, respectively. Additionally, the vibrational bands at 1403 cm−1 (CSi) and 1356 cm−1 (NH bending) further confirmed the covalent bonding between carbon and silicon atoms, as well as the existence of amino groups. The distinct peaks at 1136 cm−1 and 1045 cm−1 were identified as CO (ether/hydroxyl) and SiO (siloxane) stretching modes, collectively demonstrating the coexistence of oxygen- and silicon-related functional groups on the CDs' surfaces.

XPS analysis (Fig. 2d-e, Fig. S4) provided complementary elemental and bonding information. The survey spectra confirmed that all CDs contained four elements: C, N, O, and Si, with highly consistent atomic ratios (Table S2). High-resolution C 1 s spectra were deconvoluted into four components: CSi (284.3 eV), C-C/C=C (285.0 eV), C-O/C-N (286.0 eV), and CO (288.1 eV), validating the hybrid carbon‑silicon framework and oxygen/nitrogen functionalization. The N 1 s spectra exhibited two peaks at 399.6 eV (CN) and 400.8 eV (NH), corroborating the FTIR-derived amine/amide configurations. For O 1 s spectra, three contributions were resolved: CO (531.0 eV), CO (531.8 eV), and SiO (532.8 eV), while the Si 2p spectra displayed dual peaks at 101.4 eV (CSi) and 102.2 eV (SiO), which aligned with the FTIR-detected SiO and CSi vibrations.

We sincerely appreciate the reviewer's constructive suggestion. In response, we have supplemented the FTIR and XPS analyses with explicit characterization of potential surface functional groups on the CDs. These comprehensive spectral data further corroborate the structural similarity in surface functional groups across all five CD variants, thereby refuting the hypothesis of surface state-dominated emission mechanisms. The specific revisions are detailed below:

The chemical composition and surface functionalization of the five CDs were systematically investigated by FTIR and XPS analyses. FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2c) identified consistent surface functionalization across all samples. A broad absorption band at 3428 cm-1 was attributed to the overlapping stretching vibrations of OH (hydroxyl) and NH (amine/amide) groups, indicating the presence of hydrophilic surface moieties. The peak at 2945 cm-1 corresponded to CH stretching vibrations in aliphatic or aromatic carbon structures. Two prominent absorption bands at 1698 cm-1 and 1629 cm-1 were assigned to the stretching modes of CO (carbonyl) and CN (amine/amide) bonds, respectively. Additionally, the vibrational bands at 1403 cm-1 (CSi) and 1356 cm-1 (NH bending) further confirmed the covalent bonding between carbon and silicon atoms, as well as the existence of amino groups. The distinct peaks at 1136 cm-1 and 1045 cm-1 were identified as CO (ether/hydroxyl) and SiO (siloxane) stretching modes, collectively demonstrating the coexistence of oxygen- and silicon-related functional groups on the CDs' surfaces.

XPS analysis (Fig. 2d-e, Fig. S4) provided complementary elemental and bonding information. The survey spectra confirmed that all CDs contained four elements: C, N, O, and Si, with highly consistent atomic ratios (Table S2). High-resolution C 1 s spectra were deconvoluted into four components: CSi (284.3 eV), C-C/C=C (285.0 eV), C-O/C-N (286.0 eV), and CO (288.1 eV), validating the hybrid carbon‑silicon framework and oxygen/nitrogen functionalization. The N 1 s spectra exhibited two peaks at 399.6 eV (CN) and 400.8 eV (NH), corroborating the FTIR-derived amine/amide configurations. For O 1 s spectra, three contributions were resolved: CO (531.0 eV), CO (531.8 eV), and SiO (532.8 eV), while the Si 2p spectra displayed dual peaks at 101.4 eV (CSi) and 102.2 eV (SiO), which aligned with the FTIR-detected SiO and CSi vibrations.

The remarkable consistency in surface functional groups across five CDs, as evidenced by both FTIR and XPS spectra, strongly suggests that the observed emission color variations originate from differences in the carbon core electronic structure rather than surface chemistry modifications. This conclusion is further supported by the highly uniform elemental composition observed in all samples [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of full-color solid-state fluorescent carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The photophysical properties of the full-color solid-state CDs were systematically investigated to elucidate their unique luminescence behavior. The as-synthesized CDs exhibit exceptional solid-state fluorescence (Fig. S5), overcoming the ubiquitous AIQ effect that limits conventional CDs [17]. This anomalous behavior stems from spatial confinement (Si-O-Si networks suppress intermolecular π-π stacking) and electronic tuning (Expanded sp2 domains redshift emission via quantum confinement). Our spray pyrolysis method represents a transformative advancement in solid-state CD synthesis, achieving matrix-independent luminescence without post-processing or stabilizers.

High-resolution 3D PL excitation-emission mapping ( Fig. 3
a–e ) unveils single, symmetric emission centers with stable maximum wavelengths across all CDs, confirming that fluorescence originates from intrinsic carbon core states rather than surface-related effects [18]. Combining the characterization of CDs (see SI Note 1 for details) eliminated the possibility of heterogeneous surface states dominating the luminescence process. The optimal excitation/emission wavelengths were identified as 360/440 nm (B-CDs), 370/520 nm (G-CDs), 390/555 nm (Y-CDs), 400/595 nm (O-CDs), and 430/630 nm (R-CDs) ( Fig. 3
f, g). Remarkably, both solid-state and ethanol-dispersed CDs exhibited excitation-independent emission (Fig. S6), further corroborating the dominance of carbon core-derived transitions—a stark contrast to conventional CDs whose surface-state-dependent emission maxima vary with excitation wavelength due to heterogeneous functionalization.

UV–vis spectroscopy showed ( Fig. 3 h) that polychromatic solid CDs exhibited multimodal absorption spanning 200–450 nm. The absorption bands at 200–300 nm correspond to π-π* transitions in aromatic structures, while the broad absorption features at 340–460 nm (observed for B-, G-, Y-, and O-CDs) arise from n-π* transitions in sp2-hybridized domains. A systematic redshift in emission maxima accompanied by progressively intensified n-π* transition absorption was observed with increasing carbonization degree, revealing a direct correlation between sp2 domain expansion and enhanced n-π* transition probability. Intriguingly, R-CDs exhibit a distinct n-π* absorption band (310–430 nm), attributable to precursor-specific modulation of surface molecular clusters and their orbital energy levels [19].

TRPL studies ( Fig. 3
i) uncovered a strong dependence of fluorescence decay kinetics on carbonization degree. The average lifetime (τ) decreased sequentially from 7.46 ns (B-CDs) to 1.39 ns (R-CDs). This trend reflects a synchronous decline in the radiative-to-nonradiative decay rate ratio (kr/knr) with emission redshift, indicating enhanced non-radiative recombination pathways as carbonization intensifies. The extension of sp2 domains during carbonization narrows the bandgap, inducing a red shift in emission. However, excessive growth of sp2 domains concurrently introduces lattice defects and surface states, which act as non-radiative recombination centers, accelerating energy dissipation and shortening fluorescence lifetimes. The solid-state fluorescence QYs of the CDs exhibit a progressive decrease from 33.7 % for B-CDs to 5.58 % for R-CDs, as shown in Fig. S7. This distinct wavelength-dependent reduction in QY stems from two synergistic factors: First, carbonization-induced expansion of sp2 domains leads to increased defect state density, thereby shifting the luminescence mechanism from efficient intrinsic bandgap transitions in the blue region to less efficient defect-mediated transitions in the red region. Second, non-radiative recombination channels are significantly enhanced due to both the proliferation of defects and the strengthened electron-phonon coupling in larger conjugated systems.

TRPL studies in conjunction with QY measurements reveal a dual regulatory mechanism governing the optoelectronic properties of these CDs. While controlled carbonization facilitates bandgap narrowing and emission red-shifting through the systematic growth of sp2 domains, this structural evolution simultaneously introduces non-radiative recombination centers that progressively diminish luminescence efficiency. These findings elucidate the fundamental mechanism behind efficiency loss in CDs and provide a roadmap for tailoring their optoelectronic properties [20].

Collectively, our optical analyses conclusively demonstrate that the photoluminescence of these CDs is intrinsically governed by their sp2-rich carbon cores, with minimal influence from surface states. The ability to precisely tune emission across the visible spectrum through controlled carbonization and precursor design—while maintaining exceptional solid-state stability—positions these materials as promising candidates for next-generation optoelectronic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence mechanism of full-color solid-state fluorescent carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The PL mechanism and structural evolution of the multicolor CDs were elucidated through an integrated analysis of reaction dynamics and material architecture. In Fig. 4a, during spray pyrolysis, rapid dehydration and carbonization between MA and APTES drive the formation of polymer-aggregated nanoparticles, which subsequently crystallize into graphitic-core CDs. Concurrently, APTES undergoes hydrolysis-condensation to construct a Si–O–Si network that encapsulates the CDs via amide bonds [21]. This hierarchical architecture addresses two critical challenges in solid-state luminescence: (i) spatial isolation of CDs to suppress AIQ, and (ii) controlled resonance energy transfer (RET) to mediate emission redshift. The silicon‑oxygen network acts as a dielectric spacer, modulating interparticle distances to facilitate energy transfer without fluorescence loss (Fig. 4c), while surface carboxyl groups sterically hinder π-π stacking [22]. This dual mechanism—combining RET-mediated wavelength tuning and steric stabilization—enables stable, tunable solid-state emission across the visible spectrum (Fig. 4b), resolving the long-standing trade-off between color adjustability and quenching resistance in carbon-based luminophores.

TGA (Fig. S8) provided critical insights into the thermal stability and compositional hierarchy of the CDs. Three distinct degradation stages were observed: (i) water desorption (<120 °C), (ii) decomposition of organic Si–O–Si components (150–350 °C), and (iii) graphitic domain degradation (>500 °C). The consistency of these profiles across all CDs variants, despite emission-specific compositional variations, confirms the universal presence of the protective Si–O–Si network. This structural uniformity ensures maintained interfacial distances under thermal stress, explaining the exceptional fluorescence stability under prolonged illumination. The gradual mass loss at elevated temperatures correlates with sequential breakdown of the hybrid organic-inorganic architecture, defining the thermal stability limits of the engineered CDs.

Alkaline etching experiments (pH = 13.0) (Fig. S9) conclusively validated the indispensable role of the Si–O–Si network in sustaining radiative pathways. Complete fluorescence quenching upon dissolution of the silicon matrix directly links structural integrity to emission sustainability. This behavior contrasts sharply with conventional CDs, where surface passivation dominates stability, underscoring the paradigm-shifting nature of our core-matrix architecture. The rapid emission loss under alkaline conditions further suggests that the Si–O–Si network not only provides physical separation but also chemically stabilizes emissive sp2 domains against oxidative degradation.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) in a KCl electrolyte (Fig. S10) revealed fundamental electronic structure modifications governing emission tunability [23]. The calculated HOMO-LUMO energy gaps (Fig. S11) systematically narrowed from 3.12 eV (B-CDs) to 1.98 eV (R-CDs), directly correlating with the observed emission redshift (440 → 630 nm). This bandgap reduction aligns with UV–vis absorption edge displacements and TRPL lifetime trends, forming a tripartite validation of the size-dependent quantum confinement mechanism. The expanded sp2 domains in red-emitting CDs reduce exciton confinement energies while introducing intermediate defect states that facilitate longer-wavelength transitions (Fig. 4d) [24].

Based on comprehensive structural and electrochemical analyses, we propose a layered design strategy for CD synthesis: Controlled carbonization adjusts sp2 domain size and defect density for bandgap engineering. The Si–O–Si network serves as a multifunctional scaffold, providing: Spatial isolation to prevent AIQ; Chemical stabilization of emissive centers; Wavelength tuning via controlled interparticle interactions; Homogeneous heteroatom doping (N, O, Si) enhances radiative recombination through localized state creation.

This multiscale structural control distinguishes our methodology from conventional CD synthesis, which typically relies on post-synthetic surface modifications or external matrix encapsulation [25]. The ability to precisely manipulate core and surface properties in a single-step process addresses long-standing challenges in solid-state luminescent material design, positioning these CDs as promising candidates for optoelectronic applications requiring stable, matrix-free emitters [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: LED devices based on full-color solid-state carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Leveraging their tunable full-color emission and exceptional quantum yields, the solid-state CDs exhibit outstanding potential as high-performance color-conversion layers for LED applications [27]. Prior to device integration, we rigorously evaluated the environmental stability of CDs powders under operational conditions (Fig. S12): after 60 days of ambient storage, all CD variants retained >95 % of their initial PL intensity. Under continuous xenon lamp irradiation (24 h), no significant PL degradation was observed, confirming superior photobleaching resistance. The CDs maintained emission stability at elevated temperatures, demonstrating compatibility with demanding optoelectronic environments [28]. These results establish the CDs as robust, ready-to-deploy luminophores for solid-state lighting.

We engineered multicolor LEDs by drop-casting CD/PVP ethanol dispersions onto 365 nm UV chips, a simple yet scalable approach. The devices exhibited high-purity emissions with CIE coordinates of: Blue (0.17, 0.16), Green (0.27, 0.51), Yellow (0.42, 0.54), Orange (0.51, 0.49), Red (0.64, 0.36). A warm-white LED was fabricated by optimizing the blend ratio of B-, G-, and O-CDs, achieving: CIE coordinates: (0.33, 0.36), Correlated color temperature (CCT): 7100 K, Broadband emission: 350–700 nm (Fig. 5 ). O-CDs are favored over R-CDs in warm-white LED fabrication due to their superior luminous efficiency—achieved through higher quantum yield and prolonged TRPL lifetime—and cost-effective synthesis that removes the need for phloroglucinol. These combined photophysical and economic advantages establish O-CDs as the optimal material for scalable production, balancing high performance with practicality while preserving excellent color quality.
Critically, both single-color and white LEDs retained >95 % PL intensity after 72 h of continuous operation, it has enormous practical application value in solid-state lighting devices. (Fig. S13).

The developed CDs demonstrate superior performance compared to conventional systems by combining simplified fabrication (eliminating complex encapsulation steps required for traditional phosphors), exceptional stability (light bleaching resistance and temperature resistance), and excellent color purity (without additional filters). These combined advantages position our CDs as next-generation luminophores ideally suited for energy-efficient displays and smart lighting systems.

This work presents a breakthrough spray pyrolysis strategy for the one-step, solvent-free synthesis of full-color solid-state fluorescent CDs using MA and APTES as precursors. The key advances and implications are summarized as follows: The CDs exhibit unprecedented solid-state fluorescence due to carbon-core-dominated emission, overcoming the persistent challenge of AIQ. Organosilicon-derived Si–O–Si networks provide spatial confinement, suppressing π-π stacking while enabling controlled resonance energy transfer for emission tuning. Continuous emission tuning (440–630 nm) was achieved through precise control of carbonization temperature and precursor stoichiometry. The method eliminates post-synthetic purification, external matrices, and solvents—a paradigm shift from conventional CD synthesis. Demonstrated industrial-scale viability with high yields (>80 %) and exceptional stability (95 % intensity retention after 60 days). Fabricated multicolor LEDs and white LEDs (CIE 0.33, 0.36; CCT 7100 K) with >95 % operational stability over 72 h, validating real-world applicability. This study bridges fundamental research (elucidating structure-property relationships in CDs) and industrial translation (scalable synthesis and device integration). The strategy opens new avenues for designing high-performance carbon nanomaterials for photonics, energy-efficient lighting, and beyond.

Lin Lan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhenfeng Gan: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yuan Ma: Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Rui Bao: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xin Kong: Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Liang Liu: Supervision, Investigation. Jianhong Yi: Supervision, Resources, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant nos. 52174345), the Yunnan Science and Technology Projects (grant nos. 202401AT070367) for their support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.164408.
